project_row.name: GenLayer
project_row.category: AI and blockchain infrastructure - decentralized AI adjudication
project_row.ecosystem: EVM-compatible; official ecosystem pages list LayerZero, ZKsync, Base and BNB Chain
project_row.key_backers: North Island Ventures, Arrington Capital, Node Capital, Tykhe Block Ventures, Magnus Capital

brief.how_it_grows:
Current growth efforts focus on builders, validators and ecosystem contributors. Developer documentation, a Claude Code Skills plugin and Studio tooling lower the cost of experimenting with Intelligent Contracts. 

Builder rewards are structured as a share of points generated by deployed contracts, validators receive per-decision compensation and community contributors are offered GenLayer Points. The Foundation lists grants and ecosystem partners. 

What remains unproven is whether these programs sustain recurring developer activity after the testnet phase.
brief.whats_cooking:
GenLayer is building a network for executing Intelligent Contracts: Python-style programs that can process natural language, access web data and use AI models during execution.

Outcomes are evaluated by randomly selected validators through Optimistic Democracy, with appeals and slashing built into the network design. 

GenLayer is currently in testnet. The portal reports more than 200 builders and over one million decisions, but these remain project-reported testnet metrics with no independent verification.
brief.why_it_may_matter:
Developers, AI-consensus researchers and teams building prediction markets, insurance applications, autonomous-agent coordination or dispute systems may find GenLayer useful as a reference architecture for non-deterministic decision-making without relying on a single centralized adjudicator. 

The current opportunity is to evaluate Intelligent Contracts on testnet and study its multi-model validation design. Key unknowns include adversarial reliability, validator diversity, cost and production-scale demand. 

Watch for mainnet delivery, independently built applications, appeal activity and published security reviews.
brief.how_it_makes_money:
Users pay transaction and execution-related fees when Intelligent Contracts are processed, and disputes may require appeal bonds. 

Validators receive rewards or transaction-related compensation for evaluating outcomes. The economic mechanism is documented, but live fee demand, validator profitability and any revenue retained by the protocol or its operating organizations are not yet publicly observable.

watch_next: Clarke milestone in Q3 2026: autonomous network operations.
Publication of the GenLayer Tokenomics whitepaper.
Change in portal-reported decisions/day or transactions/day trends.
New audit or security disclosure, if any.

project_row.name: Perpl
project_row.funding_summary: Not independently confirmed; reporting-aggregator total raised shows $9.25M
project_row.key_backers: Dragonfly Capital, Brevan Howard, Mirana Ventures, L1 Digital, HashKey Capital

brief.how_it_grows:
Perpl operates several growth mechanisms at once. Users earn Perpl Points from platform activity, while a separate mPoints campaign rewards weekly trading volume with MON over a 16-week period. 

Referral rebates and fee-sharing boosts encourage existing traders to bring in additional users, and Monad ecosystem visibility supports distribution. 

The durability question remains whether activity persists after the MON campaign ends, because current evidence shows motivation and reward design rather than post-incentive retention.
brief.whats_cooking:
Perpl is a perpetual futures exchange on Monad, presented as an on-chain CLOB rather than a centralized matching engine. 

A live trading interface and active MON incentive campaign suggest current product activity. Independent dashboards show observable perp volume and related protocol metrics on Monad, while on-chain claim verification remains limited to official sources rather than independently captured execution evidence. 

The distinction worth noting is documented on-chain architecture versus still-limited external operational confirmation.
brief.why_it_may_matter:
Perpl may matter to traders already active on Monad who want additional rewards tied to trading activity, and to observers studying whether incentive-driven perp volume sustains itself after rewards end. 

Current available mechanics include Perpl Points, mPoints linked to MON rewards and referral fee sharing. Users still face leverage risk, liquidation risk, smart-contract risk and platform risk, while the future value of Perpl Points remains uncertain. 

Watch whether liquidity depth, fee revenue and trading activity hold after the 16-week MON campaign period ends.
brief.how_it_makes_money:
Traders pay maker or taker fees when opening and closing perpetual positions. Official docs describe trading, insurance and protocol-related fee components, while independent aggregator evidence confirms that protocol revenue and fee components are publicly tracked. 

Main gap: the exact amount ultimately retained by Perpl after rebates, insurance allocations and other distributions is unresolved, and current aggregator revenue evidence contains a source conflict between reported values. 

Public evidence tracks the mechanism, but net protocol take remains unclear.

warnings: Revenue evidence has unresolved source conflict; the exact gross protocol revenue value and exact metric period boundaries are unresolved and must not be stated as a single verified figure in public draft.
watch_next: Published fee schedule or revenue report.
Material security disclosure or exploit report.
Material change in Monad ecosystem status.

project_row.name: Pixie Chess
project_row.category: Web3 gaming - blockchain chess with collectible and tournament mechanics
project_row.key_backers: Paradigm, SeedClub, Trevor McFedries, Jordi Hays

brief.how_it_grows:
Pixie Chess targets chess players, collectors and tournament participants through special-ability pieces, competitive formats, prize pools and simplified onboarding. 

Auctions, packs and piece burning are designed to create repeat collection and tournament-entry loops. 

Early traction evidence is limited beyond campaign activity, and there is no clear indication yet of recurring retention beyond collectible demand or prize-seeking play.
brief.whats_cooking:
Pixie Chess is a blockchain-based chess game on Base where players collect tokenized pieces with abilities beyond standard chess rules. 

New pieces are released daily through auctions and packs, and players can burn pieces to activate them in tournaments or keep them for ladder play. The game supports Quickplay, private matches and tournaments, with email, social or Base-compatible wallet onboarding. 

Core mechanics are live and documented, but independent player and usage metrics are not yet available.
brief.why_it_may_matter:
Pixie Chess may be relevant to chess players and on-chain game designers exploring whether collectible ownership can add meaningful strategy to a skill-based game. 

Its special-ability pieces, burn-to-enter tournaments and player-funded prize pools create a concrete experiment combining chess, trading-card mechanics and blockchain assets. Key questions are whether the game remains competitive rather than pay-to-win, whether new players can enter affordably and whether tournaments generate recurring participation. 

Watch for active players, repeat play, tournament cadence and independently observable on-chain activity.
brief.how_it_makes_money:
Player spending enters through piece auctions and packs, and official docs state that money spent on pieces directly funds tournament prize pools. 

This currently appears closer to a player-funded competition economy than demonstrated team or protocol revenue. Secondary coverage reports an initial prize vault funded by piece sales and a team contribution. 

It remains unclear whether Pixie Chess retains fees, royalties or another protocol revenue share.
